fullText: The Gospel of Mark: Who, When, and Why January 25, 2025 Who actually wrote the Gospel of Mark? When? And Why? In my previous post I laid out the major themes and emphases of Mark’s Gospel, and now I want to turn to some of the key historical issues about it. I begin with the author. The two most important things to note are (1) every surviving manuscript that preserves a title ascribes the book to Mark, either calling it “The Gospel according to Mark” or “The Holy Gospel according to Mark,” or just “According to Mark” and (2) these manuscripts were produced over three centuries after the book was placed in circulation. Our oldest two manuscripts (Sinaiticus and Vaticanus, for you fellow Bible nerds) come from toward the end of the fourth century (around 375 CE), and they have the titles (“According to Mark”). What about manuscripts before then? We just have no information (since these are our two earliest). But it does mean that some 300 years after Mark had been circulating, scribes copying it were entitling it that. And how much earlier than that? Two hundred years? Fifty years? Three years? Take your guess. It’s no surprise that scribes were giving it this title by this time, since outside the manuscripts, the book was ascribed to Mark much earlier. John Mark is mentioned in the book of Acts as a Jew from Jerusalem; he comes to be associated with both Peter and Paul. Already by the early second century we hear of a story that Mark was Peter’s secretary (interpreter? translator?), who listened to him preach, and then wrote down his (Peter’s) version of what Jesus said and did. The story first comes to us in Papias, a Christian author from around 130 CE. There are two major problems with thinking that Papias’s statement demonstrates that Mark wrote this book. One is that there is no way of knowing that Papias was referring to the book we have. He mentions a Gospel; he says Mark produced it from Peter’s teachings; but he doesn’t say what is in it or quote it – so there’s no way of knowing for certain that he’s talking about our Mark. I’m not just being overly skeptical here. As I pointed out in my post on Matthew, the only two things Papias says about the Gospel Matthew allegedly wrote are not true of our Matthew. In that case he either appears to be referring to a different book altogether to shows his own information is not reliable. So too with Mark? The other problem the quotations of Papias in later sources (we don’t have his writings, only scattered references to them later) recounts traditions about Jesus that almost no one thinks can be accurate. (See this recent post: https://ehrmanblog.org/is-the-gospel-of-mark-papias-refers-to-our-gospel-of-mark/ ). If he’s not trustworthy when we can test his claims then we have to be cautious in trusting ones we cannot test. It was not for another fifty years or so that anyone definitively called this Gospel “Mark.” In his work Against Heresies, from 180 CE or so, Irenaeus names the book Mark and quotes it, so we know he’s talking about our Mark. [[Earlier authors who appear to quote Mark (e.g., Justin in 150 CE) don’t name its author (oddly)]]. If we look for any evidence in the Gospel itself that it was written by Mark or from provides Peter’s perspective on Jesus, there’s really nothing there. The author never names himself or gives any hints about his identity or indicates that he had any association with Peter or with any of the other characters in the story. He is fully anonymous. Lots of the accounts in the Gospel have nothing to do with Peter and include lots of things that Peter would not have known (e.g., what Jesus prayed in the Garden of Gethsemane when Peter was not near him and was sound asleep! 14:32-42). Peter is not portrayed in a positive light in the Gospel: he cannot understand who Jesus is, he puts his foot in his mouth, he denies him three times, and at one point Jesus calls him Satan. That doesn’t mean Peter could not be the source of the stories, but there’s nothing in the stories to make one suspect he is; just the contrary. The other thing to point out is that if the historical Mark was from Jerusalem, as Acts indicates, he almost certainly could not have written this kind of subtle and elaborate account in Greek. His native language would have been Aramaic. From the entire first century we have only one Jewish author from Judea-Galilee who has left us any Greek writings, Josephus, a high-level, highly educated, elite aristocrat. Mark would not have been in that league, if he knew any Greek at all (he may have been able to speak some if is his parents had money and he was from Jerusalem, as Acts indicates. But learning to write compositions in antiquity took many years of training for the elite kids, and to do so in a second language was highly unusual.) Why was it then attributed to Mark? When the Gospels – all of them anonymous – were assigned names, these, the four most popular, were attributed to two of Jesus’ own disciples (Matthew and John) and to intimate companions of his two most important apostles, Luke the companion of Paul and Mark the companion of Peter. Two are by members of the twelve; two are backed by the most important leaders of the early church: these are four names that guarantee the truth of the accounts. As to when it was written: obviously before Irenaeus (180 CE) cites it and Justin (150 CE) who probably knows it, more important, obviously before Matthew and Luke (80-90 CE) who both used it. There are also good indications that Mark was writing after the Jewish uprising against the Romans (66-70 CE). He indicates that that God brought about the destruction of Jerusalem (by gentiles) because Jews had rejected Jesus their messiah (thus the Parable of the Vineyard, 12:1-11; see especially 12:9), and that the temple itself had been destroyed (13:2). Usually, then, this Gospel is dated to just after the war, maybe 70-75 CE. There have been a number of suggestions as to why the author wrote the book. Some have thought that since the entire generation of eyewitnesses and disciples had died out or were soon to do so, he wanted to make a record of what had happened in Jesus’ life. It may be that he was well aware of numerous stories about Jesus in circulation and thought they should be recorded. Possibly he know many of the stories conflicted with one another and wanted a definitive account. There is no solid evidence that he knew of or even used previous written accounts that no longer survive, thought it is certainly possible. On top of these plausible speculations, one needs to consider the themes of the account as I described them in the previous post. Mark’s ultimate goal is to explain that Jesus was definitely the messiah sent from God even though he is not the one anyone expected. That is why, in Mark’s account, no one – not his family, the Jewish leaders, or even his own disciples – can understand him. Jesus repeatedly declares he has to die for others and not even his closest intimates can get their minds around it. That’s because it is the opposite of what God’s messiah was expected to do. Mark writes his Gospel to counter the Jewish claims that Jesus could not be the Messiah, showing that they are precisely the ones who don’t understand what God has done to fulfill of his age-old plan.. Note: Jesus ministry begins with the fulfillment of Scriptural prophecy in the coming of John the Baptist and ends with Jesus quoting Psalm 22 on the cross. It is all according to plan. The idea that Jesus is the messiah who must suffer and die is hugely enigmatic idea just Jesus in his ministry in mark is hugely enigmatic. He intentionally teaches so people will not understand and repent; he who unsuccessfully tries to keep his activities secret; he does not allow those who begin to understand to tell others what they’ve seen and heard; and in the end not even his disciples have figured him out: they never even learn that he has been raised from the dead. Mark may have written this account the way he did precisely because it was a perspective that nearly everyone had trouble believing: God’s messiah had to suffer, die, and be raised from the dead.

title: What Is The New Testament?  Can You Actually Say?
summary: If someone standing behind you in a long line in the grocery store should lean over and out of the blue ask: “Hey, can you tell me what the New Testament is”, what would you say? (This happens to you every day, right?)
textStart: What Is The New Testament? Can You Actually Say? January 12, 2025 What exactly is the New Testament? If someone standing behind you in a long line in the grocery store should lean over and out of the blue ask: “Hey, can you tell me what the New Testament is”, what would you say? (This happens to you every day, right?) This person wants it in one sentence. Well – come up with something. What would you say? (Try to formulate something before reading any further.) I’m not sure what I would say, but I would have a ton of options in my head, depending on what I thought she wanted really to know and on what kind of mood I was in (probably a foul one, if I’m in a long line). But among the options, here would be a relatively decent one: “The New Testament is the collection of twenty-seven books thought to be written by the apostles of Jesus that came to be considered Christian Scripture.” If she wants either clarification or more information, I or you could go from there. In this (long!) thread that I am hereby starting, I will be trying to convey concise and hopefully accurate information about the New Testament, devoting a number of posts to each of the twenty-seven books, all of them beginning with a one-sentence summary and going from there. The point of the one-sentence opening is that everyone who claims a familiarity with the books of the Bible should be able to give a succinct summary statement of what it is that is both reasonably accurate but also distinctive. That is, the summary should indicate what makes this particular book stand out from all the other books of the New Testament (so that your one sentence about Matthew is not almost identical to your once sentence about Luke; or your one sentence about 1 Thessalonians isn’t just like the one about Philippians). fullText: What Is The New Testament? Can You Actually Say? January 12, 2025 What exactly is the New Testament? If someone standing behind you in a long line in the grocery store should lean over and out of the blue ask: “Hey, can you tell me what the New Testament is”, what would you say? (This happens to you every day, right?) This person wants it in one sentence. Well – come up with something. What would you say? (Try to formulate something before reading any further.) I’m not sure what I would say, but I would have a ton of options in my head, depending on what I thought she wanted really to know and on what kind of mood I was in (probably a foul one, if I’m in a long line). But among the options, here would be a relatively decent one: “The New Testament is the collection of twenty-seven books thought to be written by the apostles of Jesus that came to be considered Christian Scripture.” If she wants either clarification or more information, I or you could go from there. In this (long!) thread that I am hereby starting, I will be trying to convey concise and hopefully accurate information about the New Testament, devoting a number of posts to each of the twenty-seven books, all of them beginning with a one-sentence summary and going from there. The point of the one-sentence opening is that everyone who claims a familiarity with the books of the Bible should be able to give a succinct summary statement of what it is that is both reasonably accurate but also distinctive. That is, the summary should indicate what makes this particular book stand out from all the other books of the New Testament (so that your one sentence about Matthew is not almost identical to your once sentence about Luke; or your one sentence about 1 Thessalonians isn’t just like the one about Philippians). But to be able to summarize a book – or a sub-collection of the books (e.g., “the Gospels” or “the Pauline epistles)” – in a sentence presupposes far more substantial bit knowledge about it. It is the tip of a large iceberg. It would take a long series of books to unpack the entire NT (“The world itself would not be able to contain the books” John 21:25), but in this post I will try to do it in a few hundred words, just to give the very basics. First: the term “New Testament.” What does it mean? The Greek word “testament” can also mean “covenant,” even though the two English words have different nuances. A “covenant” is usually a kind of legal agreement between two parties with obligations on both sides; a “testament” is usually a legal expression of the will of one party in connection with others. In the “Old Testament” (as the Christians started calling the Hebrew Bible once they had a “New Testament”) there are a number of “covenants” that God makes with his people Israel, including the one given through Moses in the Torah, the Jewish law. The people were to keep the law God gave and in return he would protect them and promote their welfare. In the prophet Jeremiah there is a prophecy of a future “New” covenant between God and his people, written on their hearts instead of on tablets of stone (Jeremiah 31:31). When Christians arrived on the scene, they declared that this “new covenant” had now been made through the death and resurrection of Jesus. This is the new “agreement” or “will of God for his people.” The record of this new covenant/testament is found in the earliest Christian writings that deal with a variety of topics: the life, death, and resurrection of Jesus (the Gospels), the history of God’s involvement with the followers of Jesus (the book of Acts), the implications of the new covenant for the beliefs and behavior of Jesus’ followers (the epistles), and the final outcome of all God’s dealings with humans at the end of the age (the book of Revelation). The collection of books that were thought to present these matters authoritatively by the apostles of Jesus, were collected into a single book and eventually called the New Covenant or the New Testament. Most of these 27 books were written in the first Christian century, though a couple (e.g., 2 Peter) may be a bit later. For the most part these are the earliest Christian writings we have, though a couple of books not in the canon are probably earlier than some that made it in (1 Clement and the Didache – both in the collection known as the Apostolic Fathers – are probably from 95-100 CE). Paul’s authentic letters were the first NT books to be written (50-62 CE or so); the Gospels are later (usually dated 70-95 CE); most of the others are very difficult to date accurately or convincingly. The books are all ascribed to apostles – either the actual disciples of Jesus (Peter, Matthew, John), his brothers (James, Jude), other apostles (Paul), or those who were closely connected with apostles (Mark, Luke). Most of these ascriptions are debated among scholars, except for seven letters of Paul that are almost certainly ones he wrote. A number of the books are written by authors falsely claiming to be one or another of these people (e.g., Ephesians, 1 Peter, James); others of them were written anonymously and were only later ascribed by readers to an apostolic author (e.g., all four Gospels; Acts). The twenty-seven books were not considered Scripture when they were first written or even in the first decades of their circulation; and only later were formed into a definite “canon” of Scripture. While some sub-collections were widely accepted by the end of the second century (the four Gospels), the entire collection of these 27 (and only these 27) did not emerge until the end of the fourth century and become more or less definitive only later (fifth century or so). The collection was never ratified by vote, but simply emerged as a wide consensus. The New Testament can be grouped into four sub-groupings, two of which consist, well, of only one book (OK, it’s odd to call these “groups”). Still, this is a helpful way to think of the canon in terms of genre and theme: Gospels: The Beginnings of Christianity (in the life, death, and resurrection of Jesus; 4 books) Matthew Mark Luke John Acts: The Spread of Christianity (through the work of Jesus’ apostles; 1 book) The Acts of the Apostles Epistles: The Beliefs, Practices, and Ethics of Christianity (in writings by apostles; 21 books) Pauline Epistles (both authentic and disputed), 13 books Romans 1 and 2 Corinthians Galatians Ephesians Philippians Colossians 1 and 2 Thessalonians 1 and 2 Timothy Titus Philemon General Epistles, 8 books Hebrews (sometimes classified as a Pauline epistle) James 1 and 2 Peter 1, 2, and 3 John Jude Apocalypse: The Culmination of Christianity (In a description of the end of this age); 1 book The Revelation of John This schematic arrangement is somewhat simplified. All of the New Testament books, for example (not just the epistles), are concerned with Christian beliefs, practices, and ethics; and Paul’s epistles are in some ways more reflective of Christian beginnings than are the Gospels. Nonetheless, this basic orientation to the New Testament writings can at least get us started in our understanding of the early Christian literature.

title: Intriguing Questions and Attempted Answers!
summary: Here are a couple of the tricky and interesting questions I have received lately on the blog, along with my answers to them. They seem important enough to me to share more broadly for everyone’s benefit.
textStart: Intriguing Questions and Attempted Answers! November 21, 2024 Here are a couple of the tricky and interesting questions I have received lately on the blog, along with my answers to them. They seem important enough to me to share more broadly for everyone’s benefit. As you’ll see, they cover a range of topics. ****************************** QUESTION: I was wondering if in Paul’s letters themselves, if there is any concept of Jesus worship like we see in the gospels? Many examples including the word proskuneo (προσκυνέω) where it is argued Jesus is being worshiped in the New Testament; are these present in Paul’s letters? MY RESPONSE: PROSKUNEO is a tricky word in Greek. It is a compound verb formed of KUNEO, which means to “kiss”, and PROS which means “before” and is generally used in the sense of falling down in reverence before someone and/or to show humility in their presence (by kissing their feet?). It is indeed often translated “worship” because it is the sort of thing one does before a god, or in the Xn tradition before God or Christ. BUT, it also can be used of what people do when they come before a powerful person, a ruler, a king. It is used that way even in the NT — see, e.g., Matthew 18:26. It occurs a good bit in the Gospels — e.g., 13 times just in Matthew usually in reference to Jesus — but in Paul only once, in 1 Cor. 14:25, in reference to God. Paul certainly believed that Chris was to be worshiped, though. That much is clear, e.g., in Philippians 2:6-11, where he indicates that at the end ALL beings will bow down and worship him. ****************************** QUESTION: I noticed that Acts 3:15 seems to have a very high Christology by calling Jesus the “author of life”. That appears to be equating Jesus with God which is much higher than what’s found in
fullText: Intriguing Questions and Attempted Answers! November 21, 2024 Here are a couple of the tricky and interesting questions I have received lately on the blog, along with my answers to them. They seem important enough to me to share more broadly for everyone’s benefit. As you’ll see, they cover a range of topics. ****************************** QUESTION: I was wondering if in Paul’s letters themselves, if there is any concept of Jesus worship like we see in the gospels? Many examples including the word proskuneo (προσκυνέω) where it is argued Jesus is being worshiped in the New Testament; are these present in Paul’s letters? MY RESPONSE: PROSKUNEO is a tricky word in Greek. It is a compound verb formed of KUNEO, which means to “kiss”, and PROS which means “before” and is generally used in the sense of falling down in reverence before someone and/or to show humility in their presence (by kissing their feet?). It is indeed often translated “worship” because it is the sort of thing one does before a god, or in the Xn tradition before God or Christ. BUT, it also can be used of what people do when they come before a powerful person, a ruler, a king. It is used that way even in the NT — see, e.g., Matthew 18:26. It occurs a good bit in the Gospels — e.g., 13 times just in Matthew usually in reference to Jesus — but in Paul only once, in 1 Cor. 14:25, in reference to God. Paul certainly believed that Chris was to be worshiped, though. That much is clear, e.g., in Philippians 2:6-11, where he indicates that at the end ALL beings will bow down and worship him. ****************************** QUESTION: I noticed that Acts 3:15 seems to have a very high Christology by calling Jesus the “author of life”. That appears to be equating Jesus with God which is much higher than what’s found in Luke. Are there any debates as to whether this was original to Luke? And if not, am I correct in understanding that Luke believes that Jesus created life? MY RESPONSE: It really depends on what the phrase “author of life” means. If it means “the one who first created life,” then yup, that would be a mighty high Christology (Christ created life itself!) If it means: “the one who brought into existence eternal life” then it could be high but not an indication of pre-existence divinity (Christ made “everlasting life” possible). Since the apostles in Acts constantly preach about about how Jesus death brings salvation, and never refer to his pre-existence or role in creation, this latter (that he creates eternal life) seems to me to be the more probable meaning.. A FOLLOW-UP QUESTION Interesting. That would make sense. That said, every translation I’ve seen for Acts 3:15 translates it in a way implying that Jesus was in fact “the one who created life”. MY RESPONSE TO THE FOLLOW-UP Really? That’s interesting! The Greek term used for “author” (as in “author of life”) is an unusual word, ARCHEGON, occurring just four times in the NT (twice in Acts; twice in Hebrews), and not terribly common outside of it. It is often translated something like originator, founder, prince, first of a family. It is a compound noun formed from the word ARCHE, which means “beginning, origin” or “first cause,” HEGEOMAI, which means “to lead the way” to guide.” ARCHE can refer either to the concept of time — “the first thing to happen, the beginning” or to a human’s status “the first among us” or “the one who has first place,” that is, a “leader” or ruler.” And so the term ARCHEGON can refer to the origin or beginning of something or to human ruler or to the originating cause or author of something. One key to knowing what it means in Acts 3:15 (The ACHEGON of Life) is by considering how it is used in the NT, especially in the NEXT occurrence in Acts two chapters later, where it definitely does not mean “creator” in the sense of “one who brings something for the first time into existence.” Acts 5:31 indicates that God “exalted Christ to his right hand as the ARCHEGON (Leader) and Savior.” If God makes him this at his exaltation, then he obviously is not making him the creator of human life in the beginning. In Hebrews the term is used to indicate that Christ is the ARCHEGON of salvation through his sufferings (2:10) and the ARCHEGON of faith; in the first it appears to mean that he blazed the way of salvation and in the other that he was the pioneer of faith. I In none of these cases does it appear to mean that Christ was the one back in Genesis who created life on earth. ****************************** QUESTION: The Stoic philosophers believed that all human beings are part of the divine logos, and so we ought to love each other and direct the impulse to care for ourselves and our own outward to people who are not part of our immediate circle, and outward again to people from different neighborhoods, cities, and countries. It seems that the Stoic view can be summed up in the phrase ‘love God, and love your neighbor’. The Stoic view sounds like the altruistic love of neighbor that Jesus taught. I’m not convinced that the ethical principles taught by Jesus are so unique. It is the apocalyptic context of those teachings that is unique. On the other hand I’m not an expert in Stoic philosophy or Christian ethics. MY RESPONSE: I will be discussing the Stoic views of ethics (in relation to their views of the world and what it means to be a human) in later posts. For now: Stoics did not exactly maintain that humans are part of the logos; they believed that the divine logos is infused of all the cosmos, including especially humans. That is a big difference. Since we have “reason” (=Logos) within us, we can see the “reason” in the world and the reason for what happens in the world. Among the Stoic authors whose ethical writings we have — Seneca, Musonius Rufus, Epictetus, and Marcus Aurelius — you will not find any injunction to “love your neighbor as yourself” in the sense of meaning “love everyone, including those who are not your family, friends, or on the same socio-economic level” That is, they never maintain that you should love a stranger. Quite the contrary, Seneca, for example, specifically ridicules giving substantial sums of money to those who are destitute. So I don’t think that Stoics would have urged altruistic behavior to anyone in need, the way Jesus did. In my book I talk a good bit about all this (more than I’ll be able to on the blog).

title: An Imagined Dialogue for the Dispute Between Paul & Barnabas in Acts Part 4 by Angus Nisbet
summary: We come now to the final post by Angus Nisbet on the dispute recorded in the NT between Paul and Barnabas, in which he tries to reconstruct what may have really happened (covered up by the NT reporting). Is it possible that Barnabas actually lashed seriously into Paul and became his enemy?

Is it possible that Barnabas actually lashed seriously into Paul and became his enemy? Is it possible that Paul’s enmity with Jewish Christians is what ultimately led to his martyrdom? Read on! fullText: An Imagined Dialogue for the Dispute Between Paul & Barnabas in Acts Part 4 by Angus Nisbet November 8, 2024 We come now to the final post by Angus Nisbet on the dispute recorded in the NT between Paul and Barnabas, in which he tries to reconstruct what may have really happened (covered up by the NT reporting). Is it possible that Barnabas actually lashed seriously into Paul and became his enemy? Is it possible that Paul’s enmity with Jewish Christians is what ultimately led to his martyrdom? Read on! And Respond! And remember: you as a Platinum member not only get access to all Platinum posts written by other Platinums, but you also can write some yourself! You don’t have to be thoroughly well-versed in the field to write one! Do you have any thoughts or ideas or curiosities you would like others to see and respond to? Send us a post!! ****************************** In this fourth post on an Imagined dialogue between Paul and Barnabas for their dispute mentioned in Acts 15: 37-40, Barnabas points out to Paul in the dialogue that Paul’s words about being all things to all people (1 Cor 9: 19-23) is an example of him doing exactly what he criticized Peter and Barnabas for doing. Barnabas criticizes Paul for insensitivity and having a tendency to make enemies because of his angry combative outbursts. Paul admits to being in mortal danger from ‘false believers’ 2 Cor 11:26 (likely Jewish Christians who advocated circumcision and close adherence to the Jewish law) and a careful reading of Acts chapters 21 & 22, indeed seems to indicate that Jewish Christians were actually involved in trying to kill Paul (Acts 21:20-22, 31, 35-36; Acts 22:21-22). The crowds were probably mainly Jewish Christians, because it wasn’t at the point that he mentioned his vision of Jesus on the Road to Damascus (Acts 22:8), that they became murderous, but only when he mentioned being sent to preach to the gentiles (Acts 22:21-22). Some have speculated that Paul may actually have been martyred by Jewish Christians, an uncomfortable scenario for Christianity, which might explain why details of Paul’s martyrdom are not known. Barnabas points out that it was him who encouraged supported and defended Paul, but that Paul treated him with disrespect. He tells Paul that he is arrogant and boastful and that he should submit to the authority of the Apostles and family of Jesus who actually knew Jesus in the flesh. Using words from 2 Corinthians, Paul answers that it is no longer appropriate to regard Jesus from a worldly perspective. He acknowledges his boasting and tries to justify it (2 Cor 12:5-6, 2 Cor 10:8). He also claims that not only is he just as authoritative and just as much a ‘Hebrew’ as the Apostles, but that he has worked harder and suffered more than any of them, but also admits that not all followers of Jesus accept that he is an Apostle (1 Cor 9:2). Barnabas tells Paul that because of his continued arrogance, his lack of respect for the Apostles, his vilifying of anyone who doesn’t agree with him, his attitude to John Mark, and his failure to apologise to Peter and Barnabas, that he will be parting company with Paul. The Dispute (continued) PAUL: I don’t have to follow what James or the apostles tell me! I am not a slave to anyone! I am free and belong to no one, although I have voluntarily made myself a slave to everyone, to win as many as possible. To the Jews I became like a Jew, to win the Jews. To those under the law I became like one under the law (though I myself am not under the law), so as to win those under the law. To those not having the law I became like one not having the law (though I am not free from God’s law but am under Christ’s law), so as to win those not having the law. To the weak I became weak, to win the weak. I have become all things to all people so that by all possible means I might save some. I do all this for the sake of the gospel, that I may share in its blessings (1 Cor 9: 19-23) BARNABAS: That’s just it, Paul, you’ve now hoisted yourself on your own petard! You accused Peter and I of hypocrisy because we compromised, yet by your own reasoning, you’ve just admitted to indulging in much the same compromise and hypocrisy. Actually, it’s not about compromising in a negative sense, but rather, it’s about being sensitive to others – something you clearly have no conception of. That’s what gets you into so much trouble why you make so many enemies, including some believers in the Messiah (whom you call false believers) who are so angry that you have been telling Jewish believers to abandon the law of Moses, that they actually want to see you dead! (2 Cor 11:26; Acts 21: 20-22, 28-31 Acts 22:22-23 ). You even upset those who have come to the Messiah through your own preaching (2 Cor 7: 8) PAUL: Even if I upset the Corinthians with my letter, I do not regret it, because their sorrow led to their repentance, just as God had intended and no one was harmed in any way by me (2 Cor 7: 8-9). BARNABAS: You also seem to have also forgotten that although I am from Cyprus, I sold my land and gave the proceeds to the Ebionim and I am still am part of the Jerusalem community of believers, yet it was I who was the first from our community to accept you as a fellow worker and believer and I brought you to the apostles (Acts 9:27). I might also point out that you never mention my role in introducing you when you tell others about your first meeting with the Apostles, Peter and James (Gal 1:18) after you had been in Damascus for those 3 early years of your faith in the Messiah. Later, I also recognized your passion for communicating the truth of our Messiah when you were arguing with the teachers of the law in the synagogues in Tarsus and I brought you to the disciples in Antioch (Acts: 11:25-26), a point which you also forget to mention (Gal 1:21), when you tell others about the start of ‘your’ missionary work. Don’t you remember how I have always encouraged and supported you and how I’ve always stood up for you. You know that I that’s how I came to be called Barnabas – son of encouragement! (Acts 4:36). But you have taken me for granted, you don’t listen to me and you treated me with disrespect in public. You are arrogant and boastful, Paul, everywhere you go, proclaiming yourself to be an apostle! Who made you an apostle anyway? The Gospel was handed down by Jesus’s to the twelve apostles, especially Peter and Jesus passed on the leadership of the community to his own younger brother, James the righteous, supported by his other brothers and sisters, and to his other disciples who actually knew Jesus in the flesh. PAUL: Although we once regarded Christ from a worldly point of view, we do so no longer! From now on, we on we regard no one in that way (2 Cor 5:16). That’s why I do not think I am in the least inferior to those ‘super apostles’ (2 Cor 11: 5). BARNABAS: That’s the understatement of the first century! PAUL: Are they Hebrews? So am I. Are they Israelites? So am I. Are they Abraham’s descendants? So am I. Are they servants of Christ? (I am out of my mind to talk like this.) I am more. I have worked much harder, been in prison more frequently, been flogged more severely, and been exposed to death again and again (2 Cor 11: 22-23). Even though I may not be an apostle to others, surely I am to you! (1 Cor 9:2). Of all people, Barnabas, you know that I am a servant of Christ Jesus, called to be an apostle and set apart for the gospel of God Rom 1:1 according to the will of God (1Cor 1:1). I persevered in demonstrating among you the marks of a true apostle, including signs, wonders and miracles (2 Cor 12:12) and if I do choose to boast, I am not a fool, because I would be speaking the truth (2 Cor 12:6). Is it not written: “Let the one who boasts boast in the Lord (1 Cor 1:31). May I never boast except in the cross of our Lord Jesus Christ (Gal 6:14). So even if I boast somewhat freely about the authority the Lord gave us for building the church up, rather than tearing it down, I will not be ashamed of it (2 Cor 10:8). BARNABAS: At least you admit you boast freely, and it’s that trait along with your total lack of respect for our leaders and your vilifying of anyone who doesn’t agree with you, which the apostles and others find so objectionable in you, Paul. The way you treated me and the Apostle Peter in Antioch, without any hint of an apology to either of us as even now, as well as your continued arrogance and boasting and your derisive attitude to John Mark, is all too much for me. It’s no surprise that you make enemies everywhere you go! Well, you’ve made an enemy of me now and I won’t be defending you to the Jerusalem community any longer and I certainly won’t be coming to Asia minor with you.

title: An Imagined Dialogue for the Dispute Between Paul & Barnabas in Acts Part 3 by Angus Nisbet
summary: Now we come to the third of four posts by Platinum member Angus Nisbet on the dispute between Paul and Barnabas recorded in the book of Acts, where he maintains that Mark (as in John Mark, allegedly the author of the Gospel!) was a spy who ratted Paul out.
textStart: An Imagined Dialogue for the Dispute Between Paul & Barnabas in Acts Part 3 by Angus Nisbet November 4, 2024 Now we come to the third of four posts by Platinum member Angus Nisbet on the dispute between Paul and Barnabas recorded in the book of Acts, where he maintains that Mark (as in John Mark, allegedly the author of the Gospel!) was a spy who ratted Paul out. And it involves a connection with the Essenes who produced the Dead Sea Scrolls. Whoa! Check it out and tell us what you think! fullText: An Imagined Dialogue for the Dispute Between Paul & Barnabas in Acts Part 3 by Angus Nisbet November 4, 2024 Now we come to the third of four posts by Platinum member Angus Nisbet on the dispute between Paul and Barnabas recorded in the book of Acts, where he maintains that Mark (as in John Mark, allegedly the author of the Gospel!) was a spy who ratted Paul out. And it involves a connection with the Essenes who produced the Dead Sea Scrolls. Whoa! Check it out and tell us what you think! Remember: you as a Platinum member not only get access to all Platinum posts written by other Platinums, but you also can write some yourself! You don’t have to be thoroughly well-versed in the field to write one! Do you have any thoughts or ideas or curiosities you would like others to see and respond to? Send us a post!! ****************************** In this third post on the Imagined dialogue between Paul and Barnabas for their dispute mentioned in Acts 15: 37-40, the dialogue uses verses from Paul’s letter to the Galatians in which Paul attacks those whom he refers to as false believers whom he claimed had ‘sneaked in’ and ‘spied’ on them by subsequently reporting back to the Apostolic Christian community in Jerusalem, which resulted in Paul and Barnabas having to go to Jerusalem to defend their ministry, to James, Peter and the others. The imagined dialogue then speculates that Paul saw John Mark, who was closely connected with the Apostles in Jerusalem, as the principal spy! Some scholars believe that the early communal Christian group described at the beginning of the book of Acts may have derived from the communal Essene group, which had characteristics in common with Jesus and John the Baptist, such as an emphasis on personal righteousness, ceremonial washing (eg baptism) and a dislike of the second temple cult sacrifices. In the dispute dialogue, I hypothesise that John Mark’s mother whom Peter lived with, may have been part of the Essene community in Jerusalem, who also had strict dietary rules, which would therefore provide a rationale for John Mark having a major issue with Paul’s lax attitude towards the Jewish laws, especially dietary laws. In the dialogue, Paul defends himself against Barnabas’s accusation of failure to accept the authority of the apostles, using his New Testament claims of authority direct from the Messiah in Galatians and Thessalonians. Paul’s words from Romans about Jesus’s views on dietary laws and that strict adherence to these laws as a sign of being weak in the faith are used in the dialogue to accuse John Mark of being weak. In the dialogue, Barnabas suggests that Paul’s admission in Romans of himself struggling to keep the law (Rom 7:14-25), was why Paul put such an emphasis on formerly being a ‘slave to the law’ and on being set free as a result of the grace (undeserved merit) of the messiah in his doctrine of justification by faith. Paul claims that Barnabas was led astray by Peter (Gal 2:13) which is incorporated into the dialogue. The imagined dialogue makes use of Paul claims in Galatians that he was approved by the apostles in Jerusalem (Gal 2: 7-9), with the implication that the whole Jerusalem leadership team accepted him. In the dialogue, Barnabas argues that this was not the case. Interpolating 1 Cor 9: 3-18, Barnabas explains to Paul, in the dialogue, that the Jerusalem leadership hot shown disapproval of Paul by asking the followers of Jesus not to give money to Paul and Barnabas’s ministry because Paul had disregarded the importance of good works and obeying the Jewish law (James 2:14-26). The Dispute (continued) BARNABAS: Unfortunately, many in the Jerusalem community, including some of the Apostles and brothers of Jesus, don’t see things the way you do and still regard you with suspicion, which is why I have had to defend you to them (Acts 9:27) as well as to the larger body of believers in Jerusalem and Judea and to keep you away from them! On the other hand, John Mark has the approval of Peter and the brothers of the Lord and he is only a young man. PAUL: Yes, and when I was his age, I was advancing in Judaism beyond many of my own age among my people and was extremely zealous for the traditions of my fathers (Gal 1:14). Anyway, John Mark is a false believer, who has effectively infiltrated our ranks to spy on the freedom we have in Christ Jesus and to try and make us slaves all over again! (Gal 2:4). BARNABAS: What are you talking about, Paul? PAUL: You know exactly what I mean, Barnabas! Jonn Mark wanted us to set aside the grace of God by persuading us to go back to preaching that righteousness could be obtained through the law! If we had acceded to this, then it would imply that we believed that Christ died for nothing! (Gal 2:21). He then deserted us right at the beginning of our work in Pamphylia and went back to Peter and James, and the other apostles in Jerusalem (Acts 13:13) and told them that when we were among the Gentiles, that we were completely disregarding the Jewish Law! As you know, that caused us to be accused of being lawless and resulted in agitators from Jerusalem coming (Gal 5:12) up to discredit our work among the gentiles in Syria, Cilicia and Galatia, throwing them into confusion with another gospel, which, as I have said, is no gospel at all (Gal 1:6-7). Those agitators also accused me of being an imposter and a liar, which has resulted in worsening of the persecution that we have already suffered! As a result, you and I had to go back up to Jerusalem after fourteen years, bringing Titus with us as a shining example of an uncircumcised gentile follower of the Messiah (Gal 2:1-3) – a living seal in the Lord of our apostolic work among the gentiles (1 Cor 9:2), to defend ourselves to those esteemed as leaders (Gal 2:6). Anyway, as you will remember, we did not give in to the accusers for one moment, so that the truth of the gospel might be preserved for the gentiles. BARNABAS: Yes, but you seem to have forgotten how insensitive you were to John Mark, who has been brought up in a strict Essene Jewish household in Jerusalem, where the dietary and cleanliness laws are of prime importance. When we arrived late at those gentile houses in Perga, you didn’t even wash your hands before you started eating, and neither did you bother to make any inquiry as to whether the food you were eating had been strangled or sacrificed to idols! John Mark had also previously spoken to you about his concerns after hearing you telling those Hellenistic Jews in Salamis on Cyprus, who had just converted to the Messiah, that they were no longer required to follow the law of Moses! I fully understand his decision to go back to Jerusalem and share is concerns with Peter and the other apostles! They are the leaders of our movement, appointed by Jesus the Messiah himself and you need to bow to their authority! PAUL: The only authority I bow to is the Messiah (1 Thes 4:2; Gal 1:1) and, as for the laws on eating, Jesus himself made it quite clear that the Kingdom of God is not a matter of what you should eat or drink, but a matter of inner righteousness, peace and joy in the Holy Spirit (Rom 14:17). If John Mark’s faith in the Messiah is so weak that he still feels he has to follow all those elaborate food rules and rituals (Rom 14:1-3), I don’t judge him for that, it’s just that I cannot allow the people to whom I preach the Gospel I’ve been entrusted with (Gal 2:7; 1Thes 2:4), to be bewitched (Gal 3:1). BARNABAS: ‘Bewitched’ is a very strong word to use, Paul. Even by calling John Mark’s faith weak, you have already judged him. You have also classified him and others who don’t share your views on circumcision, as false believers! Perhaps it you who are weak, because as you have admitted, you were unable to follow the law in your days as a strict Pharisee and you kept falling into sin! Maybe that’s why you make such a big play of grace and justification by faith and why you always talk about the law of Moses as a slave master! (Rom 7:14-25). PAUL: Are you also turning away from him who called you to that Gospel which is no Gospel at all? Have even you, Barnabas, been led astray (Gal 2:13)? No one is righteous before God! (Rom 3:10) and no one will be declared righteous in God’s sight through works of the law! Rather through the law, all of us are made conscious of our sin! (Rom 3: 20). I am now not so sure I want to bring you with me on this next missionary journey! BARNABAS: You are insufferable, Paul. It was me that brought you to the Christians at Antioch and to the Apostles. PAUL: The Apostles recognized that I had been entrusted with the task of preaching the gospel to the uncircumcised, just as Peter had been to the circumcised. For God, who was at work in Peter as an apostle to the circumcised, was also at work in me as an apostle to the Gentiles. James, Peter and John, those esteemed as pillars, gave you and I the right hand of fellowship when they recognized the grace given to me. They agreed that we should go to the Gentiles, and they to the circumcised. All they asked was that we should continue to remember the Ebionim, the very thing I had been eager to do all along (Gal 2:1-10; Acts 24:17; Rom 15:26). I more than complied with all of this, Barnabas, but then later, they undermined me by sending people to the flock we had founded, telling them not to donate any money to our ministry, but insisted that all donations should only go to the Ebionim who don’t to go out and earn a living and are unable support unable to even support wives! (1 Cor 9:5). We had never asked for any money anyway, but that is beside the point. This is my defence to those who sit in judgment on me. Don’t we have the right to food and drink? Don’t we have the right to take a believing wife along with us, as do the other apostles and the Lord’s brothers and Peter? Or is it only you and I, Barnabas who lack the right to not work for a living? Don’t you know that those who serve in the temple get their food from the temple, and that those who serve at the altar share in what is offered on the altar? In the same way, the Lord has commanded that those who preach the gospel should receive their living from the gospel. But I have not used any of these rights. What then is my reward? Just this: that in preaching the gospel I may offer it free of charge, and so not make full use of my rights as a preacher of the gospel. (1 Cor 9: 3-18) BARNABAS: Your view of events is distorted by your sense of self-importance. The leaders of our movement never accepted that you were an apostle on a level with the twelve and they remained (and still remain) deeply concerned that you were and are disregarding the central tenets of Torah and teaching other Jewish believers in the Messiah to do the same! You know full well that there is some basis in your behaviour for these concerns, as we have just discussed. Furthermore, even in your own theological system, you have not fully worked out how to reconcile your extreme emphasis on grace and faith with the requirements of the Jewish law, which were fully upheld by Jesus our Messiah! The leaders warned you about going into gentile houses and eating food that might have been from animals that had been strangled and / or sacrificed to idols (Acts 15: 20-21) and they reminded you to remember to ask the gentiles to make contributions to the Ebionim communities in Judea (Gal 2:10), the members of which had sacrificed all their worldly goods for the sake of the Messiah as they wait patiently (along with us) for his imminent return as the promised Son of Man. James also warned you about disregarding the importance of good works, according to our law, in your teaching (James 2:14-26). You then flagrantly disregarded these warnings and that is why the James and the apostles believed that your teaching should no longer be supported by money from the believers. You seem to have conveniently forgotten that it is your actions that have partly led to the Apostles being concerned about your ministry! [Continued in Part 4]

title: An Imagined Dialogue for the Dispute Between Paul & Barnabas in Acts Part 2 by Angus Nisbet
summary: Here Angus Nisbet continues his discussion of Paul and Barnabas and the dispute they had, where he gets into more fascinating issues, involving what might lie behind the dispute and how non-canonical writings that did not make it into the NT might help us figure it out. There are some controversial views here.

Send us a post!! ******************************* In this second post on the imagined dialogue between Paul and Barnabas for their dispute mentioned in Acts 15: 37-40, I begin the first of 3 sections of the dispute dialogue, in which of Paul’s responses to Barnabas make use of Paul’s actual writings from his New Testament letters. In this first section of the imagined dialogue, I use Paul’s letter to the Galatians, in which he indicates that the Gospel is about freedom from slavery to the Jewish law effected by the cross. He attacks those whom he refers to as ‘agitators’ who ‘pervert’ the Gospel by insisting on the necessity of circumcision and following other aspects of Jewish law. The dialogue hypothesizes that Paul had John Mark in mind as one of these agitators! The dialogue continues with quotes from Galatians in which Paul refuses to accept Peter and James and the other Apostles in Jerusalem (with whom John Mark is closely connected) as any more authoritative on the Gospel message than Paul himself. The reason he gives is that he got his Gospel direct from Jesus by revelation. The dialogue also links the dispute with the other dispute mentioned in Galatians chapter 2, in which Paul had a public row with Peter with Peter and Barnabas in Antioch. In the imagined dialogue for the dispute over John Mark, Barnabas continues by claiming that Paul’s version of the public Antioch dispute is not accurate and he accuses Paul of never apologising to Peter for being disrespectful and also claims that he has now made an enemy of Peter. Just such a scenario was suggested by a 3rd or 4th century pseudo-epigraphical letter of Peter to James in which Peter is supposed to have written: “for some from among the gentiles have rejected my lawful preaching, attaching themselves to certain lawless and trifling preaching of the man who is my enemy” (ie Paul). 1 In the imagined dispute dialogue, Barnabas claims that Paul’s belief (stated in Gal 1:23) that the Jerusalem church fully backs him, is a misapprehension and, that in reality, he is regarded with suspicion by the Judean Christian community and thought by some to be an impostor and a liar about his claimed revelations. Using Paul’s own words from his letters to the Romans and Corinthians, he denies lying and being an impostor. The Dispute PAUL: You must be out of your mind Barnabas! I’m not taking that agitator with us again (Gal 5:12). He and the others from James, who preach circumcision, effectively cut in on the Galatians and threw them into confusion (Gal 5: 7-10). They will have to pay the penalty, because they are emptying the stumbling block of the cross of it’s power (which is the gospel)! In fact, I wish those who preach the necessity of circumferential cutting, would completely cut themselves off (Gal 5:12). BARNABAS: You seem to be deliberately punning on the word ‘cut’ and I am not quite sure what you mean. I presume that you mean that you wish that John Mark and his colleagues from Jerusalem would cut themselves off from the Christian community, but your statement is ambiguous and it sounds as if are you might be making the hateful statement that you wish that they would cut off their own male organs? PAUL: You can draw your own conclusions, Barnabas! Don’t you remember, John Mark wouldn’t even go inside the houses of the gentiles who had accepted the Gospel of Messiah Jesus and he wanted them all to be circumcised! He didn’t just desert the mission, he effectively deserted (Gal 1:6) the one who called us to live in the undeserved favour of the Messiah by trying to turn the gentiles to a different gospel – which is really no gospel at all (Gal 1:7). By perverting the gospel of the Messiah, he even seems to have confused you, Barnabas. As far as I’m concerned, anyone who preaches a gospel other than the Gospel that the gentiles accepted from us, should be cursed by God! (Gal 1:6-8). BARNABAS: How can you be so judgmental, Paul? John Mark was a follower of Jesus long before you were and he received the gospel from Peter himself and he actually met Jesus! Not everyone has the same view of what the gospel is as you do! PAUL: The gospel I preach is not of human origin. I did not receive the Gospel from any man, nor was I taught it; rather, I received it by revelation from Jesus Christ. I did not go up to Jerusalem to see those who were apostles before I was! (Gal 1:11-12 &17). As for those who were held in high esteem – (whatever they were makes no difference to me; God does not show favouritism) – they added nothing to my message (Gal 2: 6). When Peter came to Antioch, I challenged him to his face, because prior to men coming up from James in Jerusalem, he was eating with the Gentiles, but when they arrived, he reversed his stance and separated himself from the Gentiles! He was clearly giving in to those rigid Judean believers who, among their continued dietary stipulations and other slavish adherences to the Jewish law, still insist that gentiles should be circumcised to meet God’s requirements. The Antioch Jewish believers, including you Barnabas, joined in this hypocrisy and behaviour which is incompatible with the truth of the gospel! That’s why I pointed out to you and Peter, that although Jews, you had been living like gentiles, but when in the presence of those from the Jerusalem messianic community, you both switched back to living like Jews. I also pointed out to everyone there that you and Peter gave assent to the view of those circumcizers that gentiles should be made to follow the full Jewish law and customs, which both of you had ceased from following! (Gal 2:11-15). BARNABAS: That’s your version of events, Paul! Actually, Peter was merely trying to avoid upsetting those Jewish believers in the Messiah who are zealous for the law of Moses (Acts 21:20-21). You never listened to his answer to you because you were so full of your own self-righteousness. How could you treat Peter with such disrespect? He was one of the original apostles – a disciple of Jesus throughout Jesus’s whole ministry, right back to his baptism by John (Acts 1:21-22), yet from when I first brought you to the apostles (Acts 9:27), it was Peter who was prepared to listen to your perspective on the Gospel, against the advice of the brothers of Jesus and some of the other apostles. He even invited you to stay with him for two weeks and he and his family graciously looked after you (Gal 1:18). The next time you saw Peter was on this very occasion in Antioch, which was over 14 years since you last met him, but instead of expressing your gratitude to him both for his hospitality, and for the way he was prepared to listen to what you had to say in your early years in the faith, instead you publicly embarrassed and disrespected both him and me and you have still not apologised to either of us for your arrogance. As a result, you’ve made an enemy of him. PAUL: When I started preaching the Gospel, neither the twelve, nor anyone else among the Lord’s called out ones in Judea had actually met me, but they only had only heard the report about me as the man who formally persecuted them, was now preaching the faith he once tried to destroy and they praised God because of me (Gal 1:23). BARNABAS: They were never that quick to approve you, Paul and many of them were never convinced of your legitimacy. Certainly your view on how you are perceived is not how most of the followers of the way in Judea see you now!. As you know, many still regard you with suspicion and think your claim to have seen the risen Jesus is imagined, or worse still, a lie! They also think you have betrayed the law of Moses, which, as they point out, Jesus upheld to his dying day. As a result, they think that you are an imposter! (Acts 9:26; Acts 21:21; Gal: 2: 3-5) PAUL: Yes Barnabas, I have been regarded as imposter, known, yet regarded as unknown (2 Cor 6: 8), but the God and Father of the Lord Jesus, who is to be praised forever, knows that I am not lying. (2 Cor 11:31). If anyone is confident that they belong to the Messiah, they should consider again that we belong to the Messiah just as much as they do (2 Cor 10:7). I speak the truth in Christ—I am not lying, my conscience confirms it through the Holy Spirit— I have great sorrow and unceasing anguish in my heart. For I could wish that I myself were cursed and cut off from the Messiah for the sake of my people, those of my own race (Rom 9: 1-3). References https://ehrmanblog.org/a-letter-from-peter-to-james-against-paul/ [Continued in Part 3]

title: An Imagined Dialogue for the Dispute Between Paul & Barnabas in Acts Part I by Angus Nisbet
summary: Here is the first of a four-post thread for Platinum members by long-standing Platinum member Angus Nisbet. It is a terrifically imaginative discussion of Paul and Barnabas (focused on the book of Acts) that touches on a wide range of issues that we deal with a lot on the blog — Paul’s conflict with other apostles such as James; the views of Christ and salvation dominant among the earliest Jewish followers of Jesus; early Christian Gnosticism; the reliability of the book of Acts; and … more!
